title: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 — AI Management System Overview
content: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI management system (AIMS) within an organization. It is designed for organizations providing or using AI-based products or services, ensuring responsible AI development and deployment. The standard follows the harmonized management system structure (Clauses 4-10) compatible with ISO 27001, ISO 9001, and other MSS standards. It includes normative Annex A (controls), normative Annex B (implementation guidance), informative Annex C (AI objectives and risks), and informative Annex D (sector usage).

title: Clause 4 — Context of the Organization
content: The organization shall determine external and internal issues relevant to its purpose and affecting its AIMS. It shall consider the intended purpose of AI systems developed, provided, or used, and determine its roles (AI provider, producer, customer, partner, subject, authority). The scope of the AIMS shall be documented and available. Organizations must consider applicable legal requirements including prohibited uses of AI, regulatory guidelines, cultural values and ethics, and competitive landscape.

title: Clause 5 — Leadership
content: Top management shall demonstrate leadership and commitment by ensuring the AI policy and objectives are established, integrating AIMS into business processes, making resources available, communicating importance, directing persons to contribute, and promoting continual improvement. The AI policy shall be appropriate to purpose, provide framework for objectives, include commitment to requirements and improvement, be documented, communicated, and available to interested parties.

title: Clause 6 — Planning
content: The organization shall plan actions to address AI risks and opportunities, establish AI risk criteria, define and establish an AI risk assessment process (informed by AI policy, producing consistent results, identifying risks, analyzing consequences and likelihood, evaluating against criteria), define AI risk treatment process (select controls, compare with Annex A, produce Statement of Applicability), and conduct AI system impact assessments for potential consequences to individuals and societies.

title: Clause 7 — Support
content: The organization shall determine and provide resources for the AIMS. It shall ensure competence of persons doing work affecting AI performance through education, training, or experience. Persons shall be aware of the AI policy, their contribution, and implications of nonconformity. Internal and external communications shall be determined. Documented information shall be controlled with appropriate identification, format, review, and approval.

title: Clause 8 — Operation
content: The organization shall plan, implement, and control processes for operational planning. AI risk assessment shall be performed at planned intervals or when significant changes occur. AI risk treatment plan shall be implemented. AI system impact assessment shall be performed considering deployment, intended use, foreseeable misuse, specific technical and societal context, and applicable jurisdictions.

title: Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation
content: The organization shall monitor, measure, analyze, and evaluate AIMS performance. Internal audits shall be conducted at planned intervals to determine conformity and effective implementation. The internal audit programme shall consider importance of processes and previous audit results. Top management shall review the AIMS at planned intervals, considering status of actions, changes in context, performance trends, nonconformities, audit results, and improvement opportunities.

title: Clause 10 — Improvement
content: The organization shall continually improve AIMS suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness. When nonconformity occurs, the organization shall react, take action to control and correct it, evaluate need for action to eliminate causes, implement actions, review effectiveness, and make AIMS changes if necessary. Documented information on nonconformities and corrective actions shall be available.

title: Annex A — Reference Control Objectives and Controls (Overview)
content: Annex A provides normative reference controls organized into 9 domains: A.2 Policies for AI (AI policy, alignment, review), A.3 Internal Organization (roles, reporting concerns), A.4 Resources for AI Systems (documentation, data, tooling, compute, human), A.5 Assessing Impacts (process, documentation, individual/societal impact), A.6 AI System Lifecycle (responsible development, lifecycle management including requirements, design, verification, deployment, operation, monitoring, documentation, event logs), A.7 Data for AI Systems (data management, acquisition, quality, provenance, preparation), A.8 Information for Interested Parties (user documentation, external reporting, incidents, transparency), A.9 Use of AI Systems (responsible use processes, objectives, intended use), A.10 Third-Party and Customer Relationships (allocating responsibilities, suppliers, customers).

title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 Overview
content: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0), released January 2023, provides a voluntary framework for managing risks associated with AI systems. It consists of four core functions: GOVERN (cultivate risk management culture), MAP (identify and contextualize risks), MEASURE (analyze, assess, benchmark risks), and MANAGE (allocate resources to manage risks). The framework is designed to be used by organizations developing, deploying, or using AI systems regardless of sector or size.

title: GOVERN Function — Cultivate Risk Management Culture
content: GOVERN establishes and maintains the organizational structures, policies, and processes for AI risk management. Categories: GOVERN 1 (policies, processes, procedures), GOVERN 2 (accountability structures with clear roles and responsibilities), GOVERN 3 (workforce diversity, equity, inclusion, accessibility), GOVERN 4 (organizational transparency and communication), GOVERN 5 (robust engagement with relevant AI actors), GOVERN 6 (policies and procedures for third-party AI entities).

title: MAP Function — Identify and Contextualize Risks
content: MAP establishes the context for AI risk management. Categories: MAP 1 (context is established and understood — intended purpose, deployment context, legal requirements), MAP 2 (categorization of AI system — data characteristics, technical attributes, intended users), MAP 3 (AI benefits and costs are enumerated), MAP 4 (risks and impacts are identified), MAP 5 (impacts to individuals, groups, communities, organizations, and society are characterized).

title: MEASURE Function — Analyze, Assess, Benchmark Risks
content: MEASURE employs quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze, assess, benchmark, and monitor AI risks. Categories: MEASURE 1 (appropriate methods and metrics are identified and applied), MEASURE 2 (AI systems are evaluated for trustworthy characteristics — valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, fair with harmful bias managed), MEASURE 3 (mechanisms for tracking identified AI risks over time are in place).

title: MANAGE Function — Allocate Resources to Manage Risks
content: MANAGE allocates risk management resources to mapped and measured risks on a regular basis. Categories: MANAGE 1 (AI risks based on assessments and other analytical output are prioritized, responded to, and managed), MANAGE 2 (strategies to maximize AI benefits and minimize negative impacts are planned, prepared, implemented, documented, and informed by input from relevant AI actors), MANAGE 3 (AI risks and benefits from third-party entities are managed), MANAGE 4 (risk treatments including response and recovery and communication plans are in place, monitored, and documented).

title: NIST AI RMF to ISO/IEC 42001 Crosswalk
content: NIST published an official crosswalk mapping AI RMF 1.0 subcategories to ISO/IEC 42001:2023 clauses and Annex A controls. Key mappings: GOVERN 1 maps to Cl.5.2 (AI policy) + A.2; GOVERN 2 maps to Cl.5.3 + A.3.2; MAP 1 maps to Cl.4 + Cl.6.1; MAP 2 maps to A.4 + A.7; MEASURE 1-3 maps to Cl.9 + A.6.2 + A.8.4; MANAGE 1-4 maps to Cl.8 + Cl.10 + A.9 + A.10. This crosswalk enables organizations implementing one framework to efficiently satisfy requirements of the other.

title: EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) Overview
content: The EU AI Act is the world's first comprehensive AI law, entered into force August 1, 2024. It establishes a risk-based regulatory framework with four tiers: Unacceptable Risk (banned — social scoring, real-time biometric in public, subliminal manipulation), High Risk (strict requirements — AI in employment, education, law enforcement, critical infrastructure, essential services), Limited Risk (transparency obligations — chatbots, deepfakes, emotion recognition), Minimal Risk (no specific obligations — spam filters, AI games). Key enforcement dates: Feb 2025 (prohibited practices), Aug 2025 (GPAI rules), Aug 2026 (high-risk obligations), Aug 2027 (full enforcement including Annex I).

title: Article 5 — Prohibited AI Practices
content: The following AI practices are prohibited: (a) subliminal techniques or purposefully manipulative/deceptive techniques materially distorting behavior causing significant harm; (b) exploitation of vulnerabilities of specific groups due to age, disability, or socio-economic situation; (c) social scoring by public authorities; (d) AI systems making risk assessments of individuals predicting criminal offending based solely on profiling; (e) untargeted scraping of facial images from internet/CCTV for facial recognition databases; (f) emotion recognition in workplace and educational institutions except for safety/medical reasons; (g) biometric categorisation based on sensitive attributes; (h) real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement (with exceptions). Effective from February 2, 2025.

title: Article 6 — Classification Rules for High-Risk AI
content: An AI system is high-risk if it is: (1) intended to be used as a safety component of a product covered by EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, or is itself such a product; AND is required to undergo third-party conformity assessment. OR (2) falls into one of the high-risk areas listed in Annex III: biometric identification, critical infrastructure management, education/vocational training, employment/worker management, access to essential services, law enforcement, migration/asylum/border control, administration of justice and democratic processes. Exceptions apply if the system does not pose significant risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights.

title: Article 9 — Risk Management System for High-Risk AI
content: A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented, and maintained for high-risk AI systems. It shall: identify and analyse known and reasonably foreseeable risks; estimate and evaluate risks that may emerge; evaluate risks based on post-market monitoring data; adopt suitable risk management measures. Testing shall be performed to identify the most appropriate risk management measures. Residual risks shall be communicated to users. Testing shall be performed at appropriate points during development and prior to placing on market.

title: Article 10 — Data and Data Governance for High-Risk AI
content: High-risk AI systems using data for training shall be developed using training, validation, and testing data sets that meet quality criteria. Data governance and management practices shall address: design choices, data collection processes, relevant data preparation operations (annotation, labelling, cleaning, enrichment), formulation of relevant assumptions, assessment of availability/quantity/suitability of data sets needed, examination for possible biases, identification of data gaps, and appropriate measures to address them.

title: Article 11 — Technical Documentation for High-Risk AI
content: Technical documentation shall be drawn up before a high-risk AI system is placed on the market or put into service and shall be kept up to date. It shall demonstrate compliance with requirements and provide information necessary for assessment. Documentation shall contain: general description of the AI system, detailed description of elements and development process, detailed information about monitoring/functioning/control, description of appropriateness of performance metrics, detailed description of risk management system, description of changes made throughout lifecycle.

title: Article 12 — Record-Keeping (Logging) for High-Risk AI
content: High-risk AI systems shall technically allow for automatic recording of events (logs) over the system lifetime. Logging capabilities shall ensure a level of traceability appropriate to the intended purpose, enabling monitoring of operation, identification of risks, and post-market monitoring. Logs shall include: period of each use, reference database, input data, identification of natural persons involved in verification of results.

title: Article 13 — Transparency and Information to Deployers
content: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. Instructions for use shall include: identity and contact details of provider, AI system characteristics/capabilities/limitations, intended purpose, level of accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, any known or foreseeable circumstances that may lead to risks, specifications for input data, human oversight measures.

title: Article 14 — Human Oversight for High-Risk AI
content: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed with appropriate human-machine interface tools to enable effective oversight by natural persons during use. Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights. Oversight measures shall enable the individual to: fully understand the AI system's capacities and limitations, properly monitor its operation, detect and address anomalies/dysfunctions/unexpected performance, decide not to use the system or disregard/reverse/override its output, intervene on operation or interrupt the system.

title: Article 15 — Accuracy, Robustness, Cybersecurity for High-Risk AI
content: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity throughout lifecycle. Accuracy levels and relevant metrics shall be declared in instructions for use. AI systems shall be resilient to errors, faults, or inconsistencies. Technical redundancy solutions may include back-up or fail-safe plans. Cybersecurity measures shall be appropriate to the risks, addressing AI-specific vulnerabilities including data poisoning, adversarial examples, model flaws, and confidentiality attacks.

title: Article 50 — Transparency for Certain AI Systems (Limited Risk)
content: Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to directly interact with natural persons (e.g. chatbots) are designed so that persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system, unless obvious. Providers of AI systems generating synthetic content (deepfakes) shall ensure outputs are marked as artificially generated in a machine-readable format. Deployers using emotion recognition or biometric categorisation must inform exposed natural persons. Deployers using AI to generate or manipulate text published to inform the public on matters of public interest shall disclose AI generation.

title: Article 53 — Obligations for GPAI Model Providers
content: Providers of general-purpose AI models shall: draw up and keep up to date technical documentation including training and testing process and results of evaluation; draw up and keep up to date information and documentation to provide to downstream providers; put in place a policy to comply with EU copyright law; draw up and make publicly available a sufficiently detailed summary about the content used for training. Providers of GPAI models with systemic risk shall additionally: perform model evaluation, assess and mitigate systemic risks, track/document/report serious incidents, and ensure adequate cybersecurity protection.

title: CIS Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Foundations Benchmark Overview
content: The CIS OCI Foundations Benchmark provides prescriptive guidance for configuring security options in Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. It covers: Section 1 (Identity and Access Management — MFA, password policies, API keys, groups, policies), Section 2 (Networking — security lists, NSGs, VCN flow logs), Section 3 (Logging and Monitoring — audit, VCN flow logs, object storage logs, service events, notifications), Section 4 (Object Storage — public access, versioning, encryption), Section 5 (Asset Management — tagging, cost tracking). The benchmark provides Level 1 (essential) and Level 2 (defense in depth) recommendations.

title: CIS OCI Section 1 — Identity and Access Management
content: Key CIS OCI IAM recommendations: 1.1 Ensure service level admins are created to manage resources of particular service; 1.2 Ensure permissions on all resources are given only to tenancy administrator group; 1.3 Ensure IAM administrators cannot update tenancy Administrators group; 1.4 Ensure IAM password policy requires minimum length of 14 characters; 1.5 Ensure IAM password policy expires passwords within 365 days; 1.6 Ensure no API keys older than 90 days; 1.7 Ensure MFA is enabled for all users with console access; 1.8 Ensure user API keys rotate within 90 days; 1.9 Ensure all OCI IAM user accounts have a valid and current email address.

title: CIS OCI Section 3 — Logging and Monitoring
content: Key CIS OCI Logging recommendations: 3.1 Ensure audit log retention period is set to 365 days; 3.2 Ensure default tags are used on resources; 3.3 Create at least one notification topic and subscription for all regions; 3.4 Ensure VCN flow logging is enabled for all subnets; 3.5 Ensure OCI Object Storage bucket access logging is enabled; 3.6 Ensure Cloud Guard is enabled in the root compartment; 3.7 Ensure a notification is configured for Identity Provider changes; 3.8-3.17 Ensure notifications for IAM group, policy, user, VCN, route table, security list, NSG, and network gateway changes.

title: CIS OCI Section 4 — Object Storage
content: Key CIS OCI Storage recommendations: 4.1 Ensure no Object Storage buckets are publicly visible; 4.2 Ensure Object Storage buckets are encrypted with customer-managed keys (CMK) in OCI Vault; 4.3 Ensure Object Storage bucket versioning is enabled; 4.4 Ensure write-level OCI Object Storage logging is enabled for all buckets.

title: OCI AI Services for ISO 42001 Compliance
content: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure provides AI services relevant to ISO 42001 compliance: OCI Data Science (model development, notebooks, model catalog, deployments with logging), OCI Generative AI (managed LLM endpoints, fine-tuning, dedicated clusters), OCI AI Vision/Language/Speech/Anomaly Detection/Document Understanding (pre-built AI services), OCI Data Labeling (annotation for training data quality), OCI Data Safe (database security assessments, user assessments, data masking), OCI Cloud Guard (security monitoring, threat detection, recommendations), OCI KMS Vault (encryption key management with auto-rotation), OCI IAM (policies, groups, MFA, authentication policies). These services map to ISO 42001 Annex A controls for AI system inventory, data governance, access control, and monitoring.

title: Cross-Framework Compliance Mapping: ISO 42001 ↔ EU AI Act ↔ NIST AI RMF ↔ CIS OCI
content: Key cross-framework mappings for OCI AI compliance: ISO 42001 Cl.5 (Leadership) + A.2 (Policies) maps to NIST GOVERN 1-2 and EU AI Act Art.9(1) and CIS OCI Section 1 (IAM). ISO 42001 Cl.6 (Planning) + A.5 (Impact) maps to NIST MAP 1-5 and EU AI Act Art.9(2),Art.27(1). ISO 42001 Cl.8 (Operation) + A.6 (Lifecycle) maps to NIST MANAGE 1-3 and EU AI Act Art.10-12,Art.15. ISO 42001 Cl.9 (Performance) + A.8 (Transparency) maps to NIST MEASURE 1-3 and EU AI Act Art.12-13 and CIS OCI Section 3. ISO 42001 A.7 (Data) maps to NIST MAP 2 and EU AI Act Art.10 and CIS OCI Section 4. ISO 42001 A.9 (Access) maps to NIST GOVERN 2 and EU AI Act Art.14 and CIS OCI Section 1. ISO 42001 A.10 (Third-Party) maps to NIST MANAGE 3, GOVERN 6 and EU AI Act Art.53.
